text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 February 2019 

Dr Amelia Reid
Riverside Medical Centre

12 Wharf Street, London, SE8 3AB
Dear Dr Amelia Reid
Michael Turner, DOB: 14-02-1991, Hospital No: H829374 NHS No. 7465129380
22A Lockside Mews, London, SE8 4PL 

Thank you for referring the above patient back to the Neurology Clinic. I reviewed him today following the birth of his child within the last six months. He reports that night-time sleep has been fragmented since the baby arrived, with multiple awakenings most nights. 

He has a diagnosis of combined generalised and focal epilepsy; he and his partner describe events consistent with both generalised convulsive seizures and focal impaired awareness episodes. He identifies occasional focal auras (brief rising epigastric sensation and sudden pause in activity) that sometimes progress, and there have been intermittent generalised tonic–clonic seizures, often out of sleep.

Regarding current seizure pattern, his seizures typically occur in clusters, generally spaced four to five days apart, though brief periods of daily seizures have been reported. VNS is used more frequently, and Buccolam was administered once each month over the past 4 months. He reports that cluster occurrence appears worse after several consecutive nights of disrupted sleep. His partner keeps a diary that broadly corroborates this pattern.

Current treatment includes Levetiracetam 1500 mg b.d., Lamotrigine 200 mg b.d., and Clobazam 10 mg nocte with an additional 5–10 mg as needed for clusters on clinician advice in the past. He has an implanted Vagus Nerve Stimulator (VNS) which he swipes more often during prodromal symptoms, particularly overnight. Rescue medication is Buccolam (midazolam) oromucosal, which he has required approximately once per month over the last four months, with good effect and no reported adverse reactions other than transient drowsiness. He denies new rashes, visual disturbance, or ataxia. He notes increased daytime fatigue, which he attributes to poor sleep and cumulative medication effects. 

Examination today was unremarkable. Neurological examination was non-focal. Observations were stable. No tongue lacerations or injuries reported since the last visit. 

We discussed the likely contribution of sleep fragmentation to seizure clustering. He remains motivated to continue VNS use and maintain a seizure diary. He has not driven. We reviewed SUDEP awareness and general safety measures, particularly around infant care and night-time supervision. He and his partner have put in place practical strategies (monitoring and alternating night duties) and feel these are helping. 

Plan:
- Continue Levetiracetam 1500 mg b.d., Lamotrigine 200 mg b.d., and Clobazam 10 mg nocte with previously advised as-needed dosing for clusters.
- Continue VNS with magnet use at aura or early cluster onset as currently practised.
- Maintain seizure and sleep diary to document cluster timing relative to sleep disruption.
- Reinforce rescue protocol with Buccolam as previously instructed; partner is confident in administration.
- Arrange ambulatory EEG to sample nocturnal periods given concern for sleep-related exacerbation.
- Refer to epilepsy specialist nurse for counselling on sleep hygiene and practical seizure management in the post-partum context.
- Safety advice reiterated; he is not driving and will continue to adhere to DVLA guidance.

I will review him in three months or sooner if clusters escalate or rescue medication is required more frequently than current use. Please let me know if there are any concerns in the interim.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: his seizures typically occur in clusters, generally spaced four to five days apart

His seizures typically occur in clusters, generally spaced four to five days apart, though brief periods of daily seizures have been reported. VNS is used more frequently, and Buccolam was administered once each month over the past 4 months

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: From the letter's 'current seizure pattern' description: it explicitly states that seizures typically occur in clusters and are generally spaced four to five days apart. This provides a clear, quantifiable interval for the ongoing pattern. The note about 'brief periods of daily seizures' indicates transient exceptions rather than a sustained current frequency. There is no stated number of seizures per cluster, so the only consistent numeric interval available to summarise current frequency is the spacing of events (clusters) every 4–5 days. Therefore, the best single normalized label is 1 per 4 to 5 day, based on the typical spacing described.
seizure_frequency_number: his seizures typically occur in clusters, generally spaced four to five days apart
